title: Lesson 320
transcript: Music playing Foundation Operational Security Concepts. This, I'm happy to report, is a very easy section. We're going to review a lot of the security concepts we've talked about already. For instance, we have talked about need to know least privilege already. This is the concept where you don't give anyone any more access than they absolutely need to do their job. And from the system administrator's perspective, they're going to look at their system. They're going to see each user in my system. What's the least amount of privilege I could give them so they could still perform their task? on my system and no more. So that's need to know, least privilege. We apply that everywhere, especially in access management. Next, separation of duties. And I'll give you an example of this, right? Separation of duties is where we take certain important processes and we separate out the tasks involved in those processes. So as an example here, let's use the check issuance process. For instance, if I was in charge of the check issuance process, and I had complete control of the process, I could do every step, I would probably be writing myself a check for $1 million every day and sticking that in the mail and sending it to myself. I should probably retake the ethics section. So check issuance is an example of where we might implement separation of duties. We'd separate out so that the first task where it might be where you fill the first person here fills in who the check is for and what the payment amount should be. The check would then be sent to the next person who would actually have to sign, validate the check. And then the check might be sent to say a third person who might be responsible for doing one final check and then actually putting that check in an envelope and putting it in the mail or activating the EFT transfer. The point is we take a critical, valuable business system process and we separate that process and have multiple people involved. What's the whole point of separation of duties? To prevent fraud. And for fraud to occur, you have to have collusion. Are you going to do this for every single business process? Absolutely not. Separation of duties is expensive. It's onerous. You're only going to do it where it's cost justified. Next, we talk about privileged account management. What's a privileged account? Well, it's a high value account, an administrator account, a root account. And what do we want to make sure that we do for these high value accounts? put more controls in place, restrict the access to only those that need that sort of access, do regular reviews of their access, use increased authentication, stuff like multi-factor authentication, and even put in place increased logging and monitoring to know exactly what these individuals are doing. job rotation. This is the idea where you rotate people through different jobs. I've got a fun animation that shows us here. I'm not going to talk about how long it took me to animate that in PowerPoint. Why do we do job rotation? A few different reasons. One, we want to cross train people. So if you have someone that's hit by a bus or something, and they were doing some critical job that no one else knew how to do, you're in real trouble. But if you've done job rotation, you've rotated people through that position, other people will know how to do it. So that's one reason. Another is it's a very good deterrent control. If you're a sketchy individual and you're doing something that you shouldn't be doing, maybe you're performing a salami attack on a financial system, If you know that someone else is going to be rotated into your job and they're going to be looking at what you've been doing, they're going to be looking at the changes you've made to the system, they're going to be essentially re-performing your job, that's a good deterrent to maybe not prevent fraud. And it's, as the situation I've just outlined implies, it's also a good detective control. When you rotate people through jobs, someone else might see something wrong. They might detect that some fraud has been occurring or some data integrity issues are occurring. So that's why we do job rotation. Let's talk about the information lifecycle. We've briefly talked about this concept yet, but the information lifecycle is made up of six phases. We first create data. As soon as data is being created, it needs to be stored somewhere. We can then use the data for various purposes, share the data with various different internal or external stakeholders. When we no longer need the data in active use, we can archive it. And then eventually, when we no longer need the data, we will destroy it. Defensible destruction. Now, we need to put controls in place that's going to protect data at all of the phases of the data lifecycle. But there's one really important thing I want to highlight that you need to do as soon as you create data. So what's the most important critical thing that you have to do at the creation step? classify the data because classification implies how valuable the data is to the organization. And it's what drives all the rest of the steps. The classification will define when we store the data. How much encryption is required? Where are we allowed to store it? Transborded data flow laws, as an example. Who's allowed to use the data? For what purposes? Who can it be shared with? How long do we have to archive it for? And do we have, say, defensible destruction requirements? So that's the data lifecycle and the types of controls that we would put in place. And remember, classify the data as soon as it's created is critically important.
